Chapter Two

Liam paced back and forth at home tower one pensively. He was on the top floor which was made of glass on all four sides. There were two towers. Each was made of a white limestone-like stone called motis. But the stone was much more malleable than any other stone on Earth and, therefore, it could bend in curvatures much like cement, except it was ten times stronger.

The high pitch ring from each step Liam took could be heard through the entire floor. There wasn’t much on the floor. A seating area was placed in the middle with two white sofas in a very modern/minimalist style and one of those tan colored pile rugs connected the two sofas on the pool of black onyx that covered the entire expanse of the floor.

In the middle of the seating area was a circular coffee table made of bronzed brass finish. On it was a leather-bound diary that Liam liked to write and read in every night before leaving, and a small tray filled with glasses and a copper flask of water. Liam could see his own reflection on the floor. That’s how clear and yet opaque the floors were. Brilliantly lucid yet with a depth that was endless. Just like Liam.

On the far end of the room, was a desk and chair flanked on the west side, closest to the row of windows where the sun would set each day, and where Liam usually spent his days and nights pondering and strategizing alone. With so much glass surrounding him, Liam had a remote that opened and closed the blinds all around for when the sun was too bright or it got too hot. Liam was very particular like that. Perhaps all that time living with Michael and Sarah rubbed off on him that way. Cool, calm, and collected but very particular and meticulous about each detail at least within the towers. With things that he had less control over, he learned to let go.

After getting tired of pacing and hearing his own footsteps, Liam stood looking down below one of the windows facing the south side. His eyes lit up. There was Jeremiah. He had come back from his mission.

Liam had sent Jeremiah as a messenger to the other kingdom to see if a truce could be reached. Both sides had been warring each other for generations. And now that Liam was an adult in his twenties with grave blue eyes and sandy colored hair, he said very little in front of others. Always collected as his father taught him to be, he never showed his thoughts, concerns and worries to others. Jeremiah was the only ear privy to half of the battles that Liam inherited, dealt with or waged. After his parents had died, he did not trust any family other than his godparents, Sarah and Michael, who were to arrive any day now as well. Liam ran to the door to greet Jeremiah as soon as he heard his footsteps.

Jeremiah was tall and of strong build with dark brown hair and amber colored eyes. A feeling of deep assurance seemed to spread across his countenance each time he smiled. He had a few lines that always furrowed when he was thinking in the middle of his forehead. And one always felt safe with him not only because of his size, but because of his ability to withstand anything with a quiet and firm demeanor. He seemed to absorb worries merely by his presence.

“So, any news?” Liam asked immediately after a short embrace of his dear friend. Liam was wearing a black pair of slacks and white dress shirt. His normally terse and contained remarks were much more excited than usual around Jeremiah this time.

“Sit, you must be tired!” said Liam.

Liam ushered Jeremiah to the sitting area and poured some water out for him and gave him a few slices of fruit, prosciutto, and cheese that he had left over when they brought him his late afternoon snack.

Jeremiah, hungry from his long trip, immediately ate up most of the food placed before him, when Liam realized he should have ordered more food.

Liam got up and spoke to Diana who sat outside of his office. She was literally the gatekeeper in more ways than one. She was always dressed in perfectly pressed tan or beige pantsuits. She wore glasses and had brown hair. Her features were plain but direct. Diana ordered dinner for the two of them.

Jeremiah finally spoke after devouring all the meat and cheese. He wasn’t much of a fruit eater.

“Liam, I am not sure if I got through them enough. Your cousin, Adal, has become even more powerful since your parents’ death. Several factions that were loyal to your father are now pledging allegiance to Adal. He seems to have convinced many on his last victory tour that you are weak. I told you after you won the battle of Klydor that you should have also done a victory tour or at least convened all the factions to determine who would support you—especially since your parents died during that battle.”

Liam winced at those words, but then collected himself almost as fast as he heard the words. “No, Jeremiah, I want Adal to think I am weak. I also want to see how many are supporting him. I only want the true loyalists on my side. And with the right training and augmented forces, we will be stronger.” He also knew deep down that if and when the prophecy came true, that they would have to act in a coordinated manner and put their differences aside. “So, for as much as animosity there is between our two kingdoms, we will both be facing the same enemy if and when the time comes.”

Jeremiah looked unconvinced but didn’t want to say anything to Liam at that moment. Liam had just lost his parents for a second time and this time, for eternity. He was beyond upset. And Jeremiah saw Liam wince when he just mentioned their death. His placid hands didn’t show it at all, but the amount of grief that he was concealing each day didn’t seem to subside. Jeremiah could see it in his eyes from time to time when he dropped down his guard.

Jeremiah did not want to disturb his mind further or question his rationale, although he didn’t believe in prophecies at all. Several minutes later, Liam looked as though some color had come back to his face that had disappeared after the discussion of his parents, and Jeremiah wanted to keep it that way.

Jeremiah stretched his arms out and yawned as the fatigue suddenly kicked in. He looked at Liam and said, “I am tired, Liam. I am going to rest some. We can speak more tomorrow about the details of my trip?”

Liam said, “Yes, I will have them send over dinner to your room.” Jeremiah nodded in agreement.

And with that, Jeremiah parted. But Jeremiah left something on the sofa.

Liam picked it up. He had seen this before. It was a dagger similar to the kind that was in his father’s journal if not the same exact dagger. Legend had it that with this dagger, the holder could see visions of the future. Only the royal bloodline could wield its power, if it was the true dagger. That meant that only he and Adal had the ability to use it. If he and Adal both passed away, then it would only work with one of Adal’s sons.

The true dagger had the ability to navigate like a GPS and a map similar to the three armbands that he hoped Kriya would bring with her. The fourth, Jeremiah was already wearing. He looked at a picture of his parents on the desk and frowned again. Then his eyes fell on the dagger once more and he thought of Kriya.

Thoughts of Kriya all these years never subsided. He always wondered what she looked like. He had his people take pictures of her unknowingly and keep tabs on her, but he told them explicitly he didn’t want to see the pictures.

The last vision of her—when they were only little children, he was forced to strike her on the shoulder so that she lay unconscious and that was something he asked forgiveness for every day. And the grief she must have been going through since then. It was the only way his parents told him at the time.

That day in the woods was like any other, except he heard a strange sound coming from the pond where the stream fed into. He went to the rocks that were edging the pool and that is where he saw the reflection of his parents in the water. He looked up and around, but there was no one there. Their reflections were still there in the water. At first, Liam thought he must be dreaming. But then he realized, they were still in the water and their reflections started glowing. He called out, “Mother, Father? Is that you?”

“Yes, son, it is us. We have come to take you back home. Your uncle is no longer a threat to you. He has been defeated. But before you can come back to us, you must make sure Kriya is not aware of where you are going. A portal will open up on the left side of this pond. But first, you must make sure Kriya does not see you at all. Otherwise, the portal will not work. This portal was made specifically for you. She cannot come through. If she tries, she will die.”

By that time, Kriya had caught up to Liam. She looked at him and asked, “Who were you talking to?”

He said, “No one. I thought I heard something, but it was nothing.” As he glanced back into the pond, the reflections were gone.

Not knowing how to make sure Kriya wouldn’t find out about anything, he first thought of telling her that her parents were calling her. That wasn’t going to work, since her parents were not due back till late evening after their flight landed from NY.

Next, he thought of saying that she needed to get something from his house, but then he thought, she would not go to his house without him being there. And both his Godparents were at work.

Next, he saw a branch and remembered a scene from a movie where if a person struck a person in a certain spot near the shoulder, they would be knocked completely unconscious. But it had to be done swiftly, accurately, and lightly enough.

So, he grabbed the branch and once he was behind Kriya, he swung. He closed his eyes at the last second. When he opened them, there lay Kriya motionless next to her bike. He checked her pulse, she was still breathing. No wounds, nothing.

Then he got onto his bike and went to the portal entrance. It was at that time that portal opened and he rode his bike into Alaysia. The path was long when he got to the other side of the portal. It was a good thing he did not relinquish his bike and decided to travel on foot instead. It would have taken him days to cut the distance without his bicycle. It was strange, he was half expecting for his parents to be on the other side. But there was no one there to receive him.

Just a voice saying, “You are on the right path, Liam. Stay on this main road and you will reach your destination at the end of this road.”

When he looked around to see who else was there and where the voice was coming from, he couldn’t discern the location or see any person nearby.

The voice then thundered, “Hurry, you must get there before nightfall. It becomes dangerous for a child after nightfall.”

“Who are you though? Where are you? Why can’t I see you?”

“When the time is right, all will be explained.”

And to this day, Liam still didn’t discern whose voice it was. But on that path was when he first saw the dagger. He was riding his bicycle as fast as he could, when suddenly on that path there was a fight that broke out between two horsemen. Liam hid his bike behind the rocks as he neared them afraid they might have seen me. But they didn’t see Liam at all. They were too engrossed in fighting each other.

It was then that he saw the dagger. It caught his eye because the handle emanated a bright blue light each time one of the horseman’s cape moved and exposed his belt holster.

Liam decided to take another path and get back onto the path at a later point so that he could make it to Alaysia’s towers before nightfall.

And now Liam was holding this same dagger after so many years had passed by. The dagger weighted him with what was to come.

It was the dagger that Liam’s father told him was going to be necessary in the future. He was to guard it with all his life until it was reunited with the four armbands/bracelets made of the same stone and forged of the same metal.

He put it in the safe that was hidden in his desk. Only he knew the combination to the first part of the lock, and the second part of the lock could only be opened afterwards with his handprint and DNA scanner. He decided not to have the retinal scanner in place until the third security lock that came up whenever anyone tried to remove anything out of the safe.

Liam spent the rest of the night perusing through his father’s journal and notes regarding the prophecy.

The next morning, Liam invited Jeremiah on a horseback run to the shoreline edges of the kingdom to see if there were any ships or invasions that they had to worry about. They had cameras installed everywhere as well as satellite imagery, but Liam liked riding horses and owned a stable with several of them. So that day he wanted to do it the old-fashioned way.

The ride was brisk that morning. The dew was still on the ground and the sky was a golden crisp autumn as the sun edged more and more of its presence onto the landscape.

“Now if only Kriya would get here,” said Liam out loud, not realizing that Jeremiah overheard him.

Jeremiah pretended to not hear him though and turned away and continued focusing on riding. He shook his head and thought:

Great, Liam still believes in that fairy tale prophecy story that Liam’s father made up for him as a child.

Jeremiah never questioned Liam or ever responded to his comments about either the prophecy or Kriya, but he also suspected that Kriya was a figment of Liam’s imagination too. There were no pictures of Kriya with Liam’s other childhood pictures on Earth. And Jeremiah found that strange.

Liam had undergone a lot of trauma as a child. God only knows what he made up in his mind as a coping mechanism, thought Jeremiah.

Liam then asked once they were completely out of town and nowhere near anyone, “Where did you get that dagger that you left on the sofa last night?”

Jeremiah couldn’t help himself this time though and said, “Liam, it’s just a dagger. Just a plain dagger. No magical dagger. OK? It was on the road somewhere and I picked it up. It means nothing. I have seen so many daggers like it being sold in that one shop in that store. I was going to give it to the lady there. She’s a friend of mine.”

“No, you cannot give it to her. It’s something I need.”

“Liam, don’t start with that prophecy talk again. I know you want to believe in it with all your heart and might. But it is just a bedtime story that your father came up with when you were little. I know you want to believe that Kriya will meet you here. But you need to grow up, Liam. You have to face reality. There is no Kriya here. This—around us, is all we have got!” Jeremiah waved his hand across the horizon on both sides and then clenched the reins of his horse a little tighter and began riding faster pulling away from Liam by several feet now.

“Let’s see how the shoreline looks today!” yelled back Jeremiah, as he was now several feet ahead of Liam.

Liam frowned. He missed his parents. Jeremiah, although his closest confidant, knew nothing about living anywhere but Alaysia. And Liam wasn’t even sure if Jeremiah, even for a second, believed that there was a Kriya at all.

Jeremiah’s parents had passed away at a young age. One time when Liam and his parents were out riding, Jeremiah, who was just a child, was peddling some furs that he had trapped and then skinned ever so expertly. He had done such an amazing job that at once Liam’s father decided to take him in when he learned that Jeremiah had no parents.

From that day on, Jeremiah became his father’s right hand. And now that his father passed away, Jeremiah is Liam’s right hand. He is the most loyal person anyone could ever ask for. However, Liam’s father did not share much but practical training with Jeremiah. Jeremiah learned everything about how to maintain the two towers’ upkeep from Liam’s father, Clovis. Clovis taught both Jeremiah and Liam how to fence, how to fight in combat, strategy and in battle, but Clovis did not teach Jeremiah the arts and humanities. Jeremiah was the most astute fighter that Alaysia had, and it was because Clovis directed him as such. And then the student surpassed the master as Jeremiah spent years perfecting his skills by learning from the best instructors that the land had to offer.

While Liam was learning to be a noble leader well versed in fighting as well as intellectual feats, the art of diplomacy etc., Clovis felt that Jeremiah would serve best as Liam’s shield. So, Clovis deliberately had Jeremiah train every day for hours perfecting his combat and military strategy skills. Clovis also only taught Jeremiah on a need to know basis.

Whereas, Clovis taught everything that he could to Liam, knowing that his time was destined to be a short one. Clovis never told Liam that he knew which day he and Isolde were going to die. He did not want to stop the inevitable because each time any leader from Alaysia tried to stop that which was foretold, not only was anyone unable to prevent it from happening but more chaos and more destruction came along with each act of defiance or each act to protect oneself from one’s fate.

As such, Clovis hid that fact from everyone including Liam’s mother, Isolde. Liam’s mother was very intelligent, more so than Clovis. Isolde also spent much of her time tutoring Liam in both the arts and sciences with Clovis. However, she was most supreme in strategy in both war as well as in peace talks. And Clovis made sure that Isolde taught Liam everything she knew in strategy before their time was up.

Jeremiah reached the shoreline first and scowled as he looked towards the horizon. He took out his binoculars to be sure. He scowled even more after he adjusted the lenses. There were ships in the distance, battleships in the water. He looked back for Liam, but he was nowhere in sight. Jeremiah took out his device and began calling all the fortresses. There were four of them, each planked in a cardinal direction relative to the central towers of Alaysia. They were very modern structures, just like the towers. The towers were like a compass’s center, grounding them and further reinforcing their directional importance. The structures also foreshadowed the rule of five. The prophecy foretold of the chosen five, each being represented by one of the five structures. Only those five together could control the structures to harness the power within them and defeat the impending enemy. The four armband wielders made up the Rule of Five. Liam was the fifth and final member. But he did not wear an armband. He didn’t have to. His power came from within.

The East Fortress was not too far off from where Jeremiah was. He had half a mind to ride up to the fortress and let Liam know when he came. But at that moment, he saw Liam.

“Adal’s at it again. This time there are three battleships on the horizon. They may be a day out depending on the weather. Take a look for yourself.” Jeremiah handed his binoculars to Liam.

Liam frowned, thinking, I guess there is no point to asking Jeremiah of relaying a message for the possibility of a truce. The time for diplomacy was long gone. Liam always knew deep down that Adal wanted Alaysia in its entirety. Years ago, the kingdom was cut in half with Liam’s father Clovis ruling what is now Alaysia, and his brother Abelard ruling the other half, now renamed as Gorlitz.

The peace was maintained during their lifetimes and both kingdoms flourished in a remarkable way than when it was solely one kingdom. It had never done so before. Everyone at the time felt it was the best decision made. There was healthy competition between the two brothers and there was also camaraderie. They were unified in times of struggle and in times of economic well-being, they continued to bring out the best in each other through healthy economic competition for the growth and progress of their kingdoms. People in both kingdoms were very happy and it was a time for peace and prosperity all around. Unfortunately, the same did not hold true after Liam and Adal took over their fathers’ relative kingdoms. There was always an animosity between Liam and Adal even as children. It was spurred on by the two kingdoms and even the kings at times, themselves.

Despite all the education and wisdom that Clovis and Isolde imparted on Liam, they could never foster camaraderie between Liam and Adal. Perhaps it was because of Adal’s selfish nature, or perhaps it was because of Liam’s anger clouding his judgment at critical times. Perhaps it was the limited foresight of Clovis and Abelard—for there were signs even during their reign of the instability to come. And then perhaps it was because of the prophecy, and Clovis’s and Abelard’s blind acceptance of it or more like their resignation to the events foretold.

Jeremiah and Liam reached the entrance of the fortress and the force field was turned off for a second as the gates were opened to let them in. It wasn’t their relative kingdoms that they had to worry about.

It was because there had been intrusions by different groups throughout the history of Alaysia—groups that had penetrated the portal or wormholes because that was the only way one could enter into Alaysia. These intrusions occurred by invaders that came from the past, present and future—and even different planets. Alaysia was always on the defensive.

Alaysia was formed by Clovis’s great-great-great-great grandfather. Strangely enough, he was from the future, year 3035. Many of the technologies were under peril because of the constant war that was being waged.

As such, they had developed a portal initially to house the weapons and train the military in relative safety. But then they realized that Alaysia could be so much more. It could be the real ‘City upon the Hill’ hidden from others—from generation to generation—except somehow this prophecy kept getting in the way no matter what was done.

A great war was predicted in Alaysia according to the prophecy, but the timing was not ever determined. It was going to be a multi-planetary war.

Both Clovis and Abelard spent years building up their defenses in anticipation of this war. The four fortresses were central to Alaysia’s defenses. There were two force fields built per fortress. One that covered a five-mile perimeter around the fortress and a separate stronger force field around the fortress itself.

The last invasion was from another planet several light years away and their weaponry was more sophisticated than Alaysia’s. But with Liam’s intelligence and honed military strategy, they were able to win, but not without severe losses. His parents died and thousands of Alaysians did as well. It was then that Liam began building more R&D centers hidden beneath the four fortresses and select locations within Alaysia.

He had a group of scientists conduct years of research on the alien technology and then emulate it. It was a success. Liam’s army now had at its disposal the same technology, only further refined. However, he did not let any of the general army or anyone know about this technology. It was researched, ready and mass produced but safely tucked away in the deep hidden chambers and tunnel system of each of the fortresses, secretly guarded and ready for use when the actual war was to be fought. He had gotten one of the major defense companies to work with Alaysia on this. Alaysia although had companies to produce products, it was not a large city like those on Earth. Everything was about efficiency and renewable energy.

A lot of the metals on Alaysia were those that were not present on Earth and had properties never witnessed by those on Earth. And with them, more things could be built, such as semiconductors that were much faster and thin as hair strands could be built, powering the smallest of things. Weapons were lighter and more targeted. Some of the weapons that they had built included a laser that when shot would kill the enemy instantly by zapping the heart only once. There was less carnage and negative side effects as a result. None of these weapons produced the chemical waste products or biohazardous material residue. There were no landmines or chemical and nuclear contamination anywhere. All such weapons that created contamination were banned from Alaysia and Gorlitz. Guns were banned in Alaysia as well. Only the lasers were used as they were considered more humane and sophisticated. The lasers could detect the age and person assigned to that laser. If another person tried to use that laser, it would not work—unless it was recalibrated by the army or the defense firm that made them.

And Liam had the full loyalty of both the army and the defense firms. Alaysia was small, and as such things were simpler in life compared to Earth. There were fewer players and stakeholders in their world compared to Earth or other planets, given that the portals kept them secure and undetected to most. There were only two kingdoms and both had similar technologies. And both recognized or at least previously recognized the necessity of inter-dependence. Liam felt that deep down, regardless of how much Adal wanted ‘all of’ Alaysia, he also knew that the co-dependence was necessary. Even if this idea was a very unconscious one for Adal.

That’s why Liam still felt this nudging feeling that there was still room for diplomacy, although Gorlitz’s battleships hung over the bay and edged closer towards Alaysia by the minute.

None of this would have been possible without my best friend, George Severson, who encouraged me to share my personal sister stories alongside the journal I kept about my sister during her 14 months of treatment. This became my book. George and I share a huge history as roommates and screen writing partners, and he and his incredible husband Brian are my daughter’s Godfathers. George has stood by me during every life high and struggle for the past twenty-six years. He is a true best friend.

Thank you to our family and friends - Tom & Rachel Glasoe, Sage Rivard, My Pham, Mary Morrison, Alan Polsky, Micheal Peterman, David Wilson, Rob Fischer, Kathy Wahlstrom, Shannon Kennedy, Lisa Gollinger, Shelly Robertson, The Marvin Family, David Cabrera, Alex Gray, Miki Garcia, Freddie Janka, John Connelly, Carl Stibolt, Don Hostler, Mark Alsbury, Laurie Klein, Allison Moore, Lori Wyard, Larka McCray, Jennifer Armetta, Dr. Stephanie Rosania, Margo & Adrian Mendez, Doug Demoor & Crew, Jo O’Connell, Martine Padilla & Crew, Maria Samano, The Pepper Family, Andrew & Birge Amondson, Susie and Todd DeRenzis, Banks Pecht, Sarah Otterstrom & Rick Rutherford, Jen Keeler and Nicole Ketring for your beauty house calls for Carolyn, Eleanor Powell and Allyson McDowell (our NYC escapes were crucial for survival), Tonya Peralta, Tami Winbury, all of the amazing realtor friends who kept me busy working, Ann Tisserand, Joseph West, Felipa Ventura, Emma Bailey, Adam Biesk & Sara Berger, Anne Linnee, Brooke Gregson, Reed & Liz Fish, Mike Dougherty, Dr. Ed Klein, Catherine Montgomery, Jacqui Burge, Joy & Rick Thompson, Erin Ellwood, The 6am Ojai Master’s Swim Folks, Our OHI HOME Team, The Oak Grove School Community, G Group and countless other friends & artists for supporting our family in the last months of Carolyn’s life…I can’t explain how much this support meant to all of us.

I’m eternally grateful to my mother, Beth Glasoe, who spent countless hours helping me edit each chapter grammatically before I shared it publicly or professionally. Together, we braved powering through an almost intolerable grief to share my lifelong story with Carolyn. Thank you to my editor, the amazing Jenny Davis, for reviewing my manuscript and helping me rearrange and improve my story. Thank you, Bruce Mason, for your editorial sounding board & continued support of Carolyn’s legacy. Countless thank yous to Lisa Casoni & Heather Stobo, Jon Smith & Christian Sidwell (who stayed with us at the end and coached us through new territory). Our friends have become family. Thank you to Carolyn’s Art Collector’s & our CGBF Board of Directors & Executive Director Freddie for helping launch The Carolyn Glasoe Bailey Foundation & The Ojai Institute.

Thank you, Chris Bailey, Carolyn’s husband, for sharing this life experience and adding clarification, pictures, and memories of our dear Carolyn for this book.

Thank you to my bonus in-laws, Joan & Bill Bailey, for your unselfish generosity to Carolyn in her time of need. We are all so blessed to have you in our lives.

Thank you to my husband, Dines Francese, who on a daily basis, teaches me generosity of spirit, joy of life, perseverance through tough times, and unconditional eternal love. I could not do this life without you.

And finally, thank you to my kids - Fliss, the most beautiful spirit I could have imagined coming into our world - who shared the most difficult moment of my life thus far - losing Carolyn. You are amazing and beautiful inside and out. Your mature spirit is invaluable, and I am reminded daily that I am so blessed to be your mom. To Matson, my bonus son, you are an amazing gift in our lives. You are our gift from Carolyn, and I can’t wait to see what you accomplish in this world with your angel mother guiding you. Writing a book about the intimate stories of life is a surreal process. This story, like her foundation, is Carolyn’s legacy. This incredible woman, my sister, will never be forgotten.
